Why Notes Agree

Part one dissected a single note: a repetition rate (pitch), a swing size (loudness), and a recipe of harmonics riding the fundamental (timbre). One note, though, is not yet music — a smoke alarm has a pitch. Music begins when notes relate. Play two together and they don't just coexist; they interact, physically, in the air and in your inner ear. Some pairs fuse into something smooth and stable. Others produce audible beating and roughness.

Musicians have names for this — consonance for the smooth pairs, dissonance for the tense ones — and music theory catalogues which intervals are which. This post focuses on the mechanism, using two facts demonstrated in the first two demos.

Fact 1 · Close frequencies fightBeats

Take two sine waves — pure tones, with no additional harmonics — and put them almost, but not exactly, on the same frequency. They drift in and out of step: in step, they add and the sound swells; out of step, they cancel and it dies. The swelling repeats at exactly the frequency difference: 

sin(2π·220·t) + sin(2π·223·t)  =  swells and dies 3 times per second

beat rate = |f₂ − f₁|        // always. no exceptions.

Press play and sweep the slider slowly from one side to the other. Near the centre: one steady tone (and watch the yellow envelope go flat). A few hertz off: a slow, almost pleasant throb — this is the wah-wah-wah you hear when a guitarist tunes two strings to each other. Ten to twenty hertz off: the throb is too fast to count and curdles into roughness, a gritty, tense texture. Past thirty or so: the roughness clears, and you suddenly hear two separate notes.

That arc — smooth → throb → rough → two notes — explains an important part of dissonance. The gritty middle zone exists because of the mechanics of your cochlea: frequencies closer than a critical band (roughly a quarter of an octave, wider for low notes) excite overlapping patches of the same membrane, and the overlap flutters. Roughness isn't a cultural judgement; it's hair cells being strobed.

Aside · Beats as a tool

Piano tuners exploit fact 1 professionally: they tune not by judging pitch but by counting beats between a string and a reference, slowing the wobble to a target rate. A tuner setting "two beats per second" is doing real-time spectral arithmetic by ear — the same subtraction your eyes are doing on the yellow envelope above.

Fact 2 · Simple ratios repeat quicklyThe interval as a knot

Now move the second tone far from the first — to musical distance. When two frequencies form a whole-number ratio, their combined wave repeats after a short time: at 3:2, every 3 cycles of one tone meet every 2 of the other, and the pattern starts over. At a ratio like 1.4983…, the pattern never quite recurs. Your ear, which part one showed is an inference machine hunting for repetition, notices the difference immediately.

The figure below makes the repetition visible. One tone drives the dot horizontally, the other vertically; the path is the Lissajous figure of the interval. A whole-number ratio means the path closes into a knot — 2:1 weaves twice across while crossing once up, 3:2 makes the next knot up the family tree. Drag the ratio slider between the knots and watch the curve precess and smear toward filling the whole square: a picture of "never repeats".

Play the interval at each snap button and listen while you look. The simple knots — 2:1 (octave), 3:2 (fifth), 4:3 (fourth) — sound stable, almost like one note. The wobblier knots — 5:4, 6:5, the thirds — are sweeter, richer, slightly less settled. This ranking, from the Pythagoreans' string ratios to every harmony textbook since, is the consonance hierarchy. But notice the demo is also a bit of a cheat: pure sine tones barely sound dissonant anywhere once they're well separated. To get the full story — and the real reason the hierarchy exists — we need the recipe from part one.

Aside · Lissajous, tuning forks, and a mirror

Jules Lissajous drew these figures in 1857 with no electronics: a tuning fork with a small mirror glued to a tine, a beam of light bounced off it onto a second mirrored fork mounted at right angles, and a wall. Two vibrations, one luminous curve. The apparatus was sensitive enough to compare frequencies to a fraction of a percent, and physics labs used "watching the knot precess" as a precision tuning method for decades.

The synthesisWhere harmonics align

Here is the step that turns two curiosities into a theory. Real notes are not sine waves — part one showed each carries a ladder of harmonics: a note at f brings f, 2f, 3f, 4f… So when two real notes sound together, it's not one pair of frequencies meeting. It's two ladders interleaving — and fact 1 applies to every pair of rungs.

Harmonic alignment reduces beating between partials. Near-misses add roughness.

Play with the figure below. The blue comb is a fixed E3's harmonics; the orange comb belongs to the second note, and the interval slider slides it along the log-frequency axis. At 3:2, look at the combs: orange rung 2 lands exactly on blue rung 3, orange 4 on blue 6 — white flares mark the alignments, and the two notes partially fuse. Now drag slightly off: every flare splits into a near-miss that pulses red — a dozen simultaneous beating pairs. That is why mistuning is especially audible on a fifth: it disturbs several strong alignments.

The red mountain range underneath is the same idea made quantitative: total roughness — every pair of partials run through a model of fact 1 (Plomp & Levelt's critical-band curve), summed — for every interval from unison to just past the octave. The valleys are not put there by hand. They fall out of the arithmetic, and they fall exactly on the simple ratios: deep valleys at 1:1, 2:1, 3:2; respectable ones at 4:3, 5:4, 6:5, 5:3. The consonance catalogue, derived. And the high plateau just past unison — and the ridge near the tritone — are the catalogue's villains, derived too.

Deep dive: the model in the mountain

For each pair of partials, the roughness contribution rises from zero (perfect unison — no beating), peaks when the spacing is about a quarter of a critical band (beats around 20–35 Hz), and dies off once the pair separates cleanly. Sethares' fit of Plomp & Levelt's 1965 listening data is a difference of two exponentials: 

roughness(f₁, f₂) = a₁a₂ · ( e^(−3.5·s·Δf) − e^(−5.75·s·Δf) )
where s = 0.24 / (0.021·fmin + 19)   // critical band width at this pitch

Sum over all 64 partial pairs of two 8-partial tones, sweep the ratio, and the figure's landscape appears. Two limitations: the model ignores fusion effects and learned expectation, which also shape judgement — physics proposes, culture disposes. And because fmin appears in the band width, the valleys flatten out at low pitch — which is precisely why bass lines avoid thirds and composers voice low chords in open fifths and octaves.

Aside · Change the timbre, change the theory

The mountain range is drawn for harmonic timbres — ladders at f, 2f, 3f… Bells, gongs, and the metallophones of a Javanese gamelan have bent ladders (their partials are inharmonic), and recomputing the same sum for those timbres moves the valleys to entirely different intervals — intervals remarkably close to the ones gamelan music actually uses. William Sethares built whole pieces on this: synthesize a timbre whose valleys sit at made-up scale steps, and the made-up scale sounds in tune. Consonance isn't in the notes; it's a contract between notes and timbre.

EpilogueThe consonance catalogue

Two facts, one synthesis: 

1. partials closer than a critical band beat; beats ~20–35 Hz read as roughness
2. simple-ratio intervals interleave their harmonic ladders rung-on-rung
⇒  the consonance hierarchy: 1:1, 2:1, 3:2, 4:3, 5:4, 6:5, 5:3 …
   and dissonance where partials narrowly miss

So the intervals music is built from are the deep valleys of a roughness landscape that your cochlea computes for free, all day, every day. But look again at the valley floor under 3:2, and a hard question surfaces. To build an instrument — a fretboard, a flute, a piano — you must pick, in advance, a fixed set of notes that lets you play these ratios from any starting note. Try to actually construct that set from the pure ratios and you will discover, as every civilization that tried did, that the arithmetic refuses: stack twelve perfect 3:2 fifths and you land almost, but not quite, back on a stack of seven 2:1 octaves. Almost. The gap has a name, the fix is a 23-cent lie spread so thin nobody can hear it, and it's the reason the piano exists.

Continued in Part three: Twelve Beautiful Lies — the spiral of fifths, the Pythagorean comma, and equal temperament: the compromise that built the modern keyboard.

Sound by Tone.js. Standing on the shoulders of: Plomp & Levelt (1965) · Sethares, Tuning, Timbre, Spectrum, Scale · Helmholtz (1863)
